# Edge persistence and a lock backtest on recorded venue data

*Auto-generated by `scripts/research_note.py` on 2026-07-21 19:57 UTC. Every number derives from `data/history/` decimal128 parquet; edges were floor-quantized at recording time, so this note can understate but not overstate.*

## Findings

1. **Executable edges are rare but real.** 0.41% of 212,252 recorded pair-ticks showed a positive lock edge after exact taker fees at displayed size (max 2.24¢ per contract).
2. **They persist far longer than an efficient market would allow.** 36 episodes on verified pairs, median lifetime 33s, longest 147 minutes of continuously executable after-fee edge. No one is bridging these venues at size.
3. **The binding constraint is capacity, not latency.** A taker entering ~82s late still captures 88% of instant-entry P&L — but taking every edge in 7 days locks only $264.90 on $97,484 deployed (0.27% absolute). Displayed depth, not reaction time, is what caps this trade at retail scale.

## Question

Cross-venue prediction-market arbitrage looks easy on a screen: the same event trades on Kalshi and Polymarket, and their prices disagree. After exact taker fees, at the size the books actually display, do executable lock edges exist — how large, how long-lived, and what could a taker at human/retail latency have captured?

## Data

- 212,252 pair-ticks across 32 curated pairs, 10,184 distinct snapshots, 2026-07-13 to 2026-07-21 (recording ongoing).
- Median snapshot cadence 16s — episode lifetimes below this are unobservable; single-tick episodes mean lifetime < cadence.
- Each tick stores executable top-of-book on both venues, the fee-adjusted per-contract lock edge, the edge at executable size, and that size (see `tinli_divergence`).

## 1. Fee-adjusted at-size edges are rare

| pair | verified | ticks | +edge ticks | share | med edge | max edge | max size |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| bdor26-kane | yes | 61 | 27 | 44.3% | 0.36¢ | 0.36¢ | 16,715 |
| fed-jul26-no-change | yes | 10,184 | 632 | 6.2% | 0.25¢ | 0.50¢ | 38,980 |
| lebron-next-gsw | **NO — trap** | 61 | 14 | 23.0% | 0.26¢ | 1.97¢ | 1,691 |
| wc26-champion-argentina | yes | 10,060 | 1 | 0.0% | 0.02¢ | 0.02¢ | 2 |
| wc26-champion-england | yes | 10,060 | 119 | 1.2% | 0.64¢ | 1.41¢ | 416 |
| wc26-champion-france | yes | 10,060 | 77 | 0.8% | 0.70¢ | 2.24¢ | 1,172 |

Across ALL 212,252 pair-ticks, 870 (0.41%) showed a positive fee-adjusted edge at displayed size. Fees erase almost every raw-basis dislocation; pairs not listed above never showed one.

## 2. When edges appear, they persist (at this cadence)

| pair | episodes | med lifetime | p90 | max |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| bdor26-kane | 1 | 1579s | 1579s | 26m |
| fed-jul26-no-change | 7 | 58s | 8842s | 147m |
| wc26-champion-argentina | 1 | 0s | 0s | 0m |
| wc26-champion-england | 6 | 293s | 732s | 12m |
| wc26-champion-france | 21 | 17s | 81s | 4m |

36 episodes on verified pairs; 10 were single-tick (true lifetime below the 16s cadence). The long episodes are the interesting finding: these books stayed crossed after fees for minutes-to-hours, consistent with thin prediction-market liquidity and no cross-venue market-making bridging them.

## 3. Lock backtest — one taker, product signal taken literally

| pair | locks | locked P&L | capital | return | ~annualized |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| bdor26-kane | 1 | $59.31 | $16,280.54 | 0.36% | 1.6% |
| fed-jul26-no-change | 7 | $176.65 | $76,716.86 | 0.23% | 6.2% |
| wc26-champion-argentina | 1 | $0.00 | $2.00 | 0.00% | 0.0% |
| wc26-champion-england | 6 | $1.57 | $610.30 | 0.26% | 29.8% |
| wc26-champion-france | 21 | $27.37 | $3,873.92 | 0.71% | 18.0% |
| **total** | **36** | **$264.90** | **$97,483.62** | **0.27%** | |

Locked profit is known at entry — a lock pays $1/contract at resolution regardless of outcome — so these are not mark-to-market P&Ls, they are settlement-guaranteed subject only to the execution assumptions below. Capital is the all-in cost (size × (1 − edge)). Annualized medians use approximate resolution dates and the same 6-hour floor as the product's lock report.

## 4. Latency decay — the edge does not wait for you

Entering k snapshots (~16s each) after an episode first appears:

| latency (ticks) | ≈ delay | locks still available | locked P&L | vs instant |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | 0s | 36 | $264.90 | 100% |
| 1 | 16s | 26 | $263.48 | 99% |
| 2 | 33s | 20 | $242.41 | 92% |
| 3 | 49s | 16 | $245.79 | 93% |
| 5 | 82s | 12 | $232.88 | 88% |
| 10 | 163s | 8 | $185.96 | 70% |

## 5. Flagged pairs showed 'edges' too — that is why they are flagged

- `lebron-next-gsw`: 14 apparent +edge ticks (max 1.97¢) — **excluded**. Resolution criteria were not verified equivalent; a 'lock' can settle both legs against you (e.g. the LeBron pairs' 8-day settlement-window gap documented in `data/event_map.yaml`).

## Assumptions and caveats

- One lock per episode at the entry tick's displayed size; a persisting edge is never re-taken (conservative on capacity).
- Fills assume the full displayed depth at both venues with no queue, slippage, or leg risk (optimistic on execution).
- Locked profit = recorded edge_at_size x size; the edge already nets both venues' exact taker fees and is floor-quantized at every stage.
- Capital = size x (1 - edge): all-in cost of a $1-payout lock.
- Only pairs human-verified at recording time are traded; flagged pairs are excluded and their apparent edges reported as traps.
- Annualized return is simple, horizon = entry -> resolution date, floored at 6 hours.
- Snapshot cadence bounds observability: sub-cadence edges are invisible, and true episode lifetimes are only bracketed.
- Resolution timestamps for annualization are approximate (settlement can lag close).
- One recording machine, one network vantage point; venue APIs were polled/streamed as documented in `docs/VENUES.md`.

*Recorder: Windows scheduled task running `scripts/snapshot.py --loop 60` since 2026-07-21 (earlier windows at similar cadence). Re-run this script as data accumulates.*
